text: Though I'm sharing a case of a sigmoid polyp, a distal sigmoid polyp, it is within a diverticulum. Our advanced gastroenterologist had made an attempt at getting it out and got the majority of it out, but part of it that was deep within the tick was still not accessible. So the two of us chose to do a combined laparoscopic-endoscopic procedure. There was no dysplasia, it was just a tubular adenoma. He's showing me right here. That's the tick that the polyp is in. Sorry, I don't have the endoscopic pictures of it, but the polyp is right in there. And the thought initially was if I could just invert this into the colon, that maybe he'd be able to just get a snare around it. And we tried this and I just wasn't able to get a good angle or to just press right on the spot well enough for him.

The surgeon performs a wedge resection of the diverticulum on the anti-mesenteric surface of the colon using cautery. The polyp is visible beneath the surgeon's left hand instrument as they excise the tissue around the diverticulum. The surgeon carefully navigates around other diverticula, cutting across them as needed for the resection.

The specimen is separated using three 5mm ports. The gastroenterologist removes the specimen through the colon via a natural orifice. The surgeon examines the defect, revealing a distal diverticulum that has been partially excised.

The surgeon divides a bridge of tissue to open the lumen of the colon, stabilizing the bowel with a 3-0 silk suture on the far lateral aspect. The defect is visible as a small opening on the left anterior aspect of the sigmoid colon. A V-lock suture is used to close the defect.

But overall, I think that this type of procedure is very beneficial to a patient because they have complete knowledge that the polyp is dealt with. This was done as an outpatient procedure. We've preserved the sigmoid colon. Yes, they are at risk of future sigmoid diverticulitis, but as I've mentioned, they've never had a episode of diverticulitis in the past. you know, really minimal morbidity to a procedure like this. Obviously the concern with closing defects like this on the bowel wall is whether or not you will narrow the lumen. I'm closing this transversely for that reason. And of course I will perform a post closure colonoscopy to ensure that my scope can get up above the lumen above the closure.

The surgeon inserts a needle through a five-millimeter trocar, causing a slight bend at the tip, but proceeds with suturing the bowel wall using an intracorporeal technique. The inner layer is sutured in an over-and-over fashion, followed by imbricating the tissue down, similar to closing the common channel in an intracorporeal anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the closure of the bowel wall in two layers using silk sutures, tying the final stitch to the stay suture positioned laterally. The surgical field is examined for bleeding and luminal narrowing, followed by a scope insertion to confirm the patency of the lumen. The proximal colon appears distended due to gas insufflation, but no leaks are observed.
